reasoning: The only scheduled FOMC meeting before the July 1, 2025 deadline is on June 18, 2025. Over the course of early 2025, the Federal Reserve has held the federal funds rate steady at 4.25%-4.5%, pointing to a resilient economy. Rate cut expectations for the June meeting have steadily declined. Initially, markets priced in a high probability (~78% in late April) for a June rate cut. However, following likely strong economic data in early May and a hawkish tone from Fed Chair Jerome Powell at the May 7 FOMC meeting ("We don't have to be in a hurry. The economy is resilient"), markets sharply recalibrated. By mid-May, sources report that fixed income markets view a June cut as unlikely, pricing the subsequent July 30 meeting as only a 50/50 chance. Market-implied odds for a June cut fell below 40% by early May and were likely pressured even lower by the May 7 FOMC outcomes. Unless there is a sudden, severe macroeconomic shock or unexpectedly weak jobs/inflation data released before mid-June, the Fed is expected to use the June meeting to update projections rather than enact a cut. The probability of an off-cycle cut or a surprise June cut is estimated at roughly 18%.
